counties.cobb-county: Cobb County sits well southeast of Rome, and Elite Catastrophe is not a daily fixture on these streets. What brings us down here is weather. When a spring or summer storm line rolls across metro Atlanta and leaves trees on roofs, across driveways, and down on power lines, our crews load up in Rome and deploy to Cobb to handle the emergency. That is the honest arrangement: a Rome-based, family-owned storm crew that answers the call 24/7 and drives to where the damage is. Cobb carries a heavy tree load, from the mature oaks over older Marietta and Smyrna neighborhoods to the leftover pines in newer south-county subdivisions, so when the wind gets serious there are plenty of targets. We work structures first: the tree through the bedroom, the limb pinning a car, the trunk blocking the only way out. Removals, trimming, and stump grinding are available too, but the reason to keep our number is the storm.

marietta.narrative: The call from Marietta usually comes after the sky has already done its worst. A summer storm pushes through Cobb County, and one of the big oaks that has shaded a street near the historic square for generations finally lets go, landing across a roof or splitting a driveway in half. That is the moment Elite Catastrophe drives for. We are a family-owned tree and storm-response crew based up in Rome, and while we are not a daily presence on Marietta streets, we deploy here when severe weather turns the county canopy into a hazard. Marietta's older neighborhoods carry some of the densest mature canopy in Cobb. Water oaks and red oaks planted decades ago now stand taller than the houses they shelter, with heavy limbs reaching over rooflines and power lines. When straight-line winds and hail hit trees like that, and the red clay underneath is already soaked from a wet spring, the failures come fast: snapped tops, uprooted trunks, and limbs hung up over the parts of the house you actually use. Our storm work in Marietta is triage before anything else. Structures come first. The tree through the roof, the limb pinning a car in the drive, the trunk blocking the only way in or out, those get handled ahead of everything cosmetic. Once the emergency is stable, we can take care of the rest: full removals of trees too damaged to save, trimming broken and hanging limbs back to safe wood, and grinding out the stumps a storm leaves behind. When the weather turns dangerous over Cobb County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7 and points the trucks toward Marietta.
marietta.treeAngle: storm-hit mature oaks near the historic square

kennesaw.narrative: Kennesaw grew up in the shadow of its mountain, and the wooded subdivisions that fill in around it are heavy with trees. That is beautiful most of the year and a real problem for about twenty minutes at a time, when a North Georgia storm line comes through and the wind funnels across the ridges. Elite Catastrophe is a Rome-based, family-owned storm-response crew, and Kennesaw is one of the Cobb County towns we deploy to when severe weather leaves trees where they do not belong. We are honest about the arrangement. We do not work these streets every day. What we do is answer the phone at two in the morning when a loblolly pine has snapped off at forty feet and come down across a bedroom, and then get on the road. The tree profile in Kennesaw's newer neighborhoods is the classic setup for wind damage. Tall pines that grew up sheltered inside a stand, then were left standing alone after construction took away the trees around them, now stand exposed in compacted red clay. Add a saturated spring and a hard gust, and those are the trees most likely to end up horizontal. Mixed in are maturing hardwoods over the older parts of town that drop heavy limbs under the same conditions. When we deploy to Kennesaw after a storm, structures come first: trees on houses, trees on cars, trees blocking a road or a driveway. From there we handle removals of what cannot be saved, cut back the broken and hanging limbs, and grind the stumps once the yard is safe to work in. The storm is the reason to call. Everything else follows it.
kennesaw.treeAngle: wind-exposed subdivision pines below Kennesaw Mountain

acworth.narrative: Of all the Cobb County towns, Acworth is the shortest drive from our home base in Rome, which matters most on the night a storm has just torn through. Acworth is lake country, wrapped around Lake Acworth and Lake Allatoona, and the wooded lots that slope down toward the water are thick with tall trees. When a spring or summer storm crosses the lake and slams into that canopy, the damage can be severe and sudden. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned crew that deploys to Acworth after severe weather. We want to be clear about what that means: we are not a local Acworth company, we are a Rome storm-response outfit that runs toward the county when the trees come down. Being the closest Cobb city to us just means we can usually get there faster. Lakeside lots bring their own hazards. Trees on a slope anchor in thinner, wetter soil, they lean toward the open water and the light, and when the wind pours across the lake they catch the full force of it. Loblolly pines uproot out of saturated red clay, mature oaks lose big limbs, and everything that falls tends to fall toward the house perched at the top of the lot. Our storm response in Acworth is structures-first every time. The tree on the roof, the limb through a window, the trunk across the only driveway down to the water, those come before anything else. Once the emergency is handled we take care of removals, trim the broken limbs back to safe wood, and grind the stumps out. When Cobb County weather turns dangerous, Elite Catastrophe runs storm calls 24/7.
acworth.treeAngle: wooded lakeside lots on Lake Acworth slopes

smyrna.narrative: Smyrna packs a lot of house and a lot of tree into a small footprint. The Jonquil City is one of the denser inner suburbs of Cobb County, with mature street trees lined up close over sidewalks, driveways, and rooftops that sit only a few feet apart. That closeness is exactly what makes storm damage here so unforgiving. When a tree comes down in Smyrna, there is rarely open ground for it to land on, so it lands on something built. Elite Catastrophe deploys to Smyrna out of Rome when severe weather hits. Smyrna is about the farthest reach of our Cobb County storm range, roughly forty-seven miles from the shop, and we make that drive because a family in a tight neighborhood with a tree through the roof needs a crew that does this work every day. We are not local to Smyrna. We are a Rome-based storm-response company that shows up after the storm. The mature street trees that give Smyrna its character are also the ones that fail into houses. Older water oaks and red oaks carry heavy limbs over rooflines, and when hail and straight-line winds work them over, or the red clay softens under a week of rain, big wood comes down in the worst possible place. Working trees off structures in a dense neighborhood is precision work: limbs come down in pieces, in the right order, with no room for a mistake between two close houses. We handle the emergency first, then removals, careful trimming, and stump grinding once the pressure is off. When the radar turns red over Cobb, Elite Catastrophe answers 24/7.
smyrna.treeAngle: close-set mature street trees over tight lots

powder-springs.narrative: Powder Springs is growing fast, and a lot of that growth happened right in among the trees. New neighborhoods here are carved out of wooded ground and set down in the heavy red clay that defines this part of Cobb County, and that combination is what makes the town a storm-damage waiting room every spring. Saturated clay loses its grip on a root plate, a straight-line wind leans on an exposed tree, and something that stood straight yesterday is on the house today. Elite Catastrophe is the crew that drives down from Rome to deal with it. Here is the honest version: we do not run Powder Springs streets day to day. We are a Rome-based, family-owned storm-response company, and we deploy to Powder Springs when severe weather leaves emergency tree work behind. The trees that give us the most trouble in these newer subdivisions are the leftover pines. They grew up crowded and slender inside a stand, then construction took away the trees around them and left them tall, thin, and exposed, rooted in clay that was graded and compacted. A wet week plus a hard gust, and those are the pines that snap or uproot onto a roof. When we get to Powder Springs after a storm, structures come first, always: the tree on the house, the limb over the front door, the trunk blocking the drive. Then we move to removals of what is too far gone, trimming the broken and hanging limbs back to safe wood, and grinding the stumps so the yard is usable again. Severe weather is the reason to call, and Elite Catastrophe takes those calls 24/7.
powder-springs.treeAngle: storm-toppled leftover pines in red-clay subdivisions

austell.narrative: When a storm parks over Austell, the town gets hit two ways at once. Wind and hail work the trees while Sweetwater Creek and the low bottoms take on water, and a tree standing in soft, saturated ground near the creek does not need much of a gust to come over. Austell mixes an older town core with newer growth on the edges, so the tree hazards run the full range, and Elite Catastrophe deploys here from Rome when severe weather turns those trees into emergencies. We are straight about what we are. Not an Austell company, but a Rome-based, family-owned storm crew that answers the call and drives to the damage 24/7. The flood-prone bottoms are the part that makes Austell different. Ground that stays wet for days holds trees loosely, and the loblolly pines and creek-side hardwoods rooted in it are prime candidates to uproot when the wind arrives on top of the water. Up in the older neighborhoods, mature oaks over close-set houses fail the way old oaks always do, shedding heavy limbs onto rooflines and cars. Whatever brought the tree down, our order of work does not change: structures first. The trunk through the roof, the limb pinning a vehicle, the tree blocking the only road out of a low-lying street, all of it ahead of anything cosmetic. Once the emergency is stable we handle removals, trim the broken limbs back to safe wood, and grind out the stumps the storm left behind. When Cobb County weather turns dangerous, Elite Catastrophe is the crew loading up in Rome and heading for Austell.
austell.treeAngle: creek-bottom trees uprooting in saturated ground

mableton.narrative: Mableton is one of Georgia's newer cities, incorporated only recently in south Cobb County, but the trees standing over it have been there a lot longer than the city limits. That mix is the whole story here: established older neighborhoods with mature canopy sitting right alongside newer subdivisions full of leftover pines. When a North Georgia storm crosses south Cobb, both kinds of tree fail, and Elite Catastrophe is the crew that deploys down from Rome to clear the wreckage. We will be honest with you about the distance and the arrangement. Mableton sits near the far edge of our storm range, close to forty-seven miles from our Rome base, and we are not a local presence on these streets. What we are is a family-owned, storm-response tree crew that answers the phone 24/7 and drives to where the damage is, however far that is. In the older parts of Mableton, mature oaks and sweetgums over settled neighborhoods drop heavy limbs and split apart under hail and straight-line wind. In the newer subdivisions, the tall pines left standing after construction lean and snap out of saturated red clay. Either way, when we roll into Mableton after a storm the priority is the same: structures first. Trees on houses, trees on cars, trees across the road or the driveway, handled ahead of anything that can wait. After the emergency is under control we take care of removals, cut the broken and hanging limbs back to safe wood, and grind the stumps so the yard comes back. The storm is what puts us on the road to Mableton, and that road stays open around the clock.
mableton.treeAngle: mixed old canopy and leftover pines in south Cobb
